doc.title: Change in estimate — prospective only
doc.event: Gild Co. bought equipment for $100,000 (10-year life, no salvage, straight-line). After 4 years of depreciation ($40,000 taken; book value $60,000), the total useful life is revised: only 5 years REMAIN. Prepare this year's depreciation entry.

variants.label: Book value forward over the new remaining life

entries.caption: Year-5 depreciation on the revised estimate

lines.label: BV ÷ new remaining life
lines.why: The undepreciated 60,000 spreads over the 5 years now expected: 12,000 per year, starting this year.
lines.trap: No catch-up, no restatement, no entry to fix the past — the old 10,000-a-year charges were RIGHT given what was known then.

lines.label: as usual
lines.why: Ordinary depreciation mechanics; only the number changed.
lines.trap: Formula: (book value − remaining salvage) ÷ REMAINING life. Using original cost or original life in either slot is the standard exam wound.

computationPaths.narration: Prospective math in three steps — everything runs from the book value at the revision date.

memorize.body: Change in ESTIMATE → prospective: (book value − salvage) ÷ remaining life, from the change year forward. Prior years untouched.
Change in depreciation METHOD (SL → DDB) = a change in estimate effected by a change in principle → still PROSPECTIVE. The one 'principle-flavored' change that doesn't restate.
The sorting rule of the whole chapter: Principle → retrospective · Estimate (and method changes) → prospective · Error → restate via prior-period adjustment.

questions.prompt: Equipment: cost 100,000, 4 years of SL taken on a 10-year life; life revised to 5 years REMAINING. New annual depreciation =

distractors.feedback: 20,000 divides the ORIGINAL cost by the new remaining life. Prospective treatment runs from BOOK VALUE: 60,000 ÷ 5 = 12,000.
10,000 is the old estimate. Estimates revise going forward — the undepreciated 60,000 now spreads over just 5 years.

build.scaffold: One computation (book value ÷ remaining life), one ordinary entry. Both decoys are restatement-thinking — estimates never touch the past.
